we had a tech issue with this drop

here's what we're going to change

- we'll redo this next week 
- won't be FCFS anymore
- all bayc/mayc holders can register for a drawing
- we'll pick 50 winners randomly (provably fair)

 based on the feedback for the drop, think this direction is more fair and will lead to happier outcome

will let everyone know 24 hours in advance of the new signup time

thank you
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
The team has identified an issue with tokenproof and is actively working on a solve.

The BAYC 5th Anniversary Collector’s Cigar Box drop has been postponed until further notice. 

We’ll announce the new timing with a minimum 24-hour notice before we go live again. https://t.co/P9uyf1UdGd
tweets.full_text:
we had a tech issue with this drop

here's what we're going to change

- we'll redo this next week 
- won't be FCFS anymore
- all bayc/mayc holders can register for a drawing
- we'll pick 50 winners randomly (provably fair)

 based on the feedback for the drop, think this direction is more fair and will lead to happier outcome

thread_tweets.text:
I would say rock n roll specifically, England (though Scotland contributed a bit) and the US are just head and shoulders above the rest of the anglosphere. you can sort of make a case for Ireland - U2, sinead oconnor, the cranberries, thin lizzy, my bloody valentine (i had them mislabeled above) 

when we get into electronic or reggae or pop, or other more recent genres, other countries start to assert themselves. 

jamaica was weirdly prolific with reggae ska and modern pop influences 

germany was massively influential as far as electronic music is concerned in the 70s 

france to me is the GOAT of electronic music/house in the 90s and 00s. daft punk, cassius, air, justice, m83, phoenix 

sweden for some reason has a bunch of producers and songwriters that churned out hit after hit. couldnt tell you why

tweets.text:
You‘re taking into account future token overhang, which is obviously sth to consider, but if you’re pricing in the future then you should also price in future developments. I can tell you there are massive things coming.

Besides, 3x multiple is way too conservative for 1) a business that is currently in exponential growth and 2) for a business where revenue essentially equals profit. What’s the multiple of Hyperliquid? Exactly.

I would underwrite your take that there is currently a TCG bubble forming and it’s questionable how sustainable this might be, fair take. But then again we wouldn’t take into account future developments and that Collector will be less dependent on it by horizontal expansion to other collectible asset classes.

Overall I think it’s a pretty midcurved take, and not sure why we should overthink one of the very few businesses with actual product market fit and explosive revenue.

Happy to revise once we’re losing momentum on the business fundamentals, but for now this is absolutely not the case.
tweets.full_text:
You‘re taking into account future token overhang, which is obviously sth to consider, but if you’re pricing in the future then you should also price in future developments. I can tell you there are massive things coming.

Besides, 3x multiple is way too conservative for 1) a business that is currently in exponential growth and 2) for a business where revenue essentially equals profit. What’s the multiple of Hyperliquid? Exactly.

I would underwrite your take that there is currently a TCG bubble forming and it’s questionable how sustainable this might be, fair take. But then again we wouldn’t take into account future developments and that Collector will be less dependent on it by horizontal expansion to other collectible asset classes.

Overall I think it’s a pretty midcurved take, and not sure why we should overthink one of the very few businesses with actual product market fit and explosive revenue.

Happy to revise once we’re losing momentum on the business fundamentals, but for now this is absolutely not the case.

3/ wave one was rules based robotics (amazon pick and pack)

wave two was computer vision, giving robots eyes

wave three is where we are now, enabling deep learning, turning sensor input (data + model) into physical actuation in real time 

the landscape is nascent https://t.co/mFPuc4FWj2

the problem is the fundamental structure of the robotic economy

*everything is financial engineering* https://t.co/B1mNIEXLDZ
thread_tweets.full_text:

thread_tweets.text:
4/ the real cost of deploying a robot is in:
- planning and site prep
- install 
- control and operations integration
- operator training
- persistent maintenance 
- upfront capex and ongoing opex 

industrial robots present a heavy ops, logistics, and financial burden
thread_tweets.full_text:
4/ the real cost of deploying a robot is in:
- planning and site prep
- install 
- control and operations integration
- operator training
- persistent maintenance 
- upfront capex and ongoing opex 

industrial robots present a heavy ops, logistics, and financial burden

thread_tweets.text:
5/ for a century, every industrial robot has lived in the system integration cost shape.

software, by contrast, has had the right cost shape for decades. 

physical AI moves robotics into the same shape as SaaS. R&D paid once. right size and right cost, shaped to fit the buyer.
thread_tweets.full_text:
5/ for a century, every industrial robot has lived in the system integration cost shape.

software, by contrast, has had the right cost shape for decades. 

physical AI moves robotics into the same shape as SaaS. R&D paid once. right size and right cost, shaped to fit the buyer.

7/ the capability gains in physical AI come from a single loop: collect data with the same hardware the robot will use, train a model tuned to that hardware’s geometry, deploy it onto a fleet, watch where it fails, retrain. 

the loop only compounds if you control every step https://t.co/KsnyIuXVWc

tweets.full_text:
1/ QUESTION: what is a century-old industry that out-precisions human laborers by 5x and has reduced manufacturing injuries by >60%?

INDUSTRIAL ROBOTS

today, industrial robotics is a tiny market. we explore why this gap exists, and why it is finally closing. https://t.co/WCq3ewX4eN

2/ read the full post here, or key data points below

https://t.co/drgNl9TRDi

to date, robots learn to move precisely along pre-programmed paths. a new lineage matters just as much - intelligence, the ability to handle a world the robot wasn’t explicitly programmed for.

3/ wave one was rules based robotics (amazon pick and pack)

wave two was computer vision, giving robots eyes

wave three is where we are now, enabling deep learning, turning sensor input (data + model) into physical actuation in real time 

the landscape is nascent https://t.co/mFPuc4FWj2

3/ but the problem isn't just capability or cost - that bottleneck is shrinking fast while capital flowing into both hardware and models is growing exponentially 

the problem is the fundamental structure of the robotic economy

*everything is financial engineering* https://t.co/B1mNIEXLDZ

4/ the real cost of deploying a robot is in:
- planning and site prep
- install 
- control and operations integration
- operator training
- persistent maintenance 
- upfront capex and ongoing opex 

industrial robots present a heavy ops, logistics, and financial burden

5/ for a century, every industrial robot has lived in the system integration cost shape.

software, by contrast, has had the right cost shape for decades. 

physical AI moves robotics into the same shape as SaaS. R&D paid once. right size and right cost, shaped to fit the buyer.

6/ every platform shift takes two inventions: 
1. a technology that makes something possible, and
2. a business model that makes it usable

one without the other is a research paper.

robotics as a service is the business model https://t.co/BwXnFD4POz

7/ the capability gains in physical AI come from a single loop: collect data with the same hardware the robot will use, train a model tuned to that hardware’s geometry, deploy it onto a fleet, watch where it fails, retrain. 

the loop only compounds if you control every step https://t.co/KsnyIuXVWc

8/ we invested in @VerneRobotics who are vertically integrating this entire physical AI stack, and we're working with them to create new models for on-demand financing

we believe every business in America deserves a robot. we believe that robot should be a Verne robot. https://t.co/9usTPLRc6F

@VerneRobotics 9/ this research was co-written by @kellyjgreer @neil_nie_ and @nolitadrip 

we're excited to continue writing + researching on the future of industrial robotics financing, including new unit economics unlocked by RaaS

tweets.text:
Codex is blowing my mind rn. Every Friday, I use Claude Code to triage/answer WhatsApp, SMS, and emails with a variety of skills. Somehow Opus 4.8 couldn't do what I was doing with 4.6 so I tried the Codex app.

It was significantly faster and better quality. I think it's not the app itself, (even if the Gmail connector is amazing) it’s the model. GPT-5.5 is remarkable.
